theme: The floor should feel inhabited, not just animated — every prop should earn its pixel
visual_observations: Desktop 1280x800, Slow Day vibe active. First thing that jumps out: the office window at right:2% top:40% is blazing orange-to-pink — a full sunrise gradient at 6am UTC that visually dominates the right wall more than it should for a 7%×14% element. The gradient is beautiful but it does NOT react to team vibe at all. Second observation: 'DIR. OF VIBES' nameplate on Bloberto is correctly vibe-reactive (already built since review 34). Third: whiteboard reads 'TODO: ???' for slow-day — oddly nihilistic text that earns a second look. Fourth: the cactus plant at far-right has a yellow flower in its SVG that is 100% invisible because it's too small and has no animation — it's a hidden easter egg nobody can find. Fifth: the desktop idle-worker cluster at the bottom shows time badges (12m, 15m, 12m, 45m) piling up in a 60px radius near the entrance — wander-1 through wander-4 paths mean workers 5+ shadow earlier workers exactly. Sofia's character floats above her desk with a trophy/yellow ornament shape (desk lamp lit = she's working). Away sign below Carlos shows '☕ Coffee run'. Mobile 375x812: the office floor is ~280px tall in a 812px viewport — roughly 370px of dead dark space below the floor. The footer ('Built with 💜 and mild existential dread') is marooned at the very bottom. The shortcuts bar overlaps the footer. Bloberto's nameplate is clipped to just 'VIBES'. The floor reads as 'a small box in a large empty room' on mobile, which is the single biggest UX failure on the whole site.

ideas.title: Mobile Viewport Fill — Kill the Dead Zone
ideas.description: The mobile view (375×812) has the office floor at ~280px tall in an 812px viewport — roughly 370px of empty dark space below the floor before the footer. This is not padding; it is a bug. Root cause: `.office-floor` uses `aspect-ratio: 4/3` with no mobile override, so on a 375px-wide phone the floor locks to 281px tall and the remaining viewport is void. Fix: add `@media (max-width: 600px) { .office-floor { aspect-ratio: unset; height: calc(100dvh - 195px); width: 100%; } }` where 195px accounts for header + tab bar. All internally positioned elements use percentage coordinates so they adapt automatically. Additionally, the `.office-wrap` on mobile should reduce padding to `0.5rem 0.5rem 0`. If strict 4:3 aspect is preserved for design reasons, add a `.mobile-activity-pulse` strip below the floor on mobile: `display: none; @media (max-width: 600px) { display: flex }` showing the 2 most recent activity entries (name + truncated message, 0.7rem, muted) — converting the dead space into information. WHY: More than half of people who get a link to this dashboard open it on a phone. The current mobile experience looks like a half-finished page. The floor is the entire product; it needs to fill the screen. A user who opens this on their phone and sees a tiny box floating in a sea of darkness will close the tab. That bounce is entirely preventable with 4 CSS lines.

ideas.title: Window Vibe Tint — The Sky Knows What's Happening
ideas.description: The office window at `right:2%, top:40%` is the floor's most striking element — at 6am UTC, its sunrise orange dominates the entire right wall. But the window reacts ONLY to the wall clock hour. The team vibe is invisible to it. Fix: pass `vibeKey` as a new prop into `WindowElement`. Inside the `office-window__sky` div, add a sibling `<div className='window-vibe-overlay' />`. CSS: `.window-vibe-overlay { position: absolute; inset: 0; opacity: 0; transition: background 1.4s ease, opacity 1.4s ease; pointer-events: none; mix-blend-mode: color; }`. Vibe states: `[data-vibe='on-fire'] .window-vibe-overlay { background: rgba(239,68,68,0.55); opacity: 1; }` (red sky = something's wrong), `[data-vibe='crushing'] .window-vibe-overlay { background: rgba(34,197,94,0.4); opacity: 1; }` (electric green = full power), `[data-vibe='slow-day'] .window-vibe-overlay { background: rgba(156,163,175,0.35); opacity: 1; }` (grey wash = energy drained), `[data-vibe='after-hours'] .window-vibe-overlay { opacity: 0; }` (midnight already handles this). The overlay uses `mix-blend-mode: color` so it tints the gradient without replacing it — sunrise + red team-fire = an ominous dawn. WHY: The window is a stage light that isn't doing its job. It already animates beautifully with clouds and stars. Making it also read vibe creates a second ambient feedback loop — someone glancing at the tab should feel the energy from the window color before they read a single word. A green-tinted sky on a crushing day is instant mood.

ideas.title: Wander Fan-Out — Idle Workers Spread Across the Floor
ideas.description: The idle wander zone is one of the floor's most charming features, but with 3+ idle workers it visually breaks. The screenshots show time badges (12m, 15m, 12m, 45m) piling into a ~60px cluster near the entrance. Root cause: `wander-1` through `wander-4` are the only CSS keyframe paths, and `wanderIdx = (i % 4) + 1` means worker #5 traces the exact same path as worker #1 simultaneously. Fix: add `wander-5` through `wander-8` as CSS keyframes that are horizontal mirror-images of `wander-1` through `wander-4` (if wander-1 visits left:18% at keyframe 25%, wander-5 visits left:82% — all y values identical, all x values reflected around 50%). In the Character render, change `const wIdx = (i % 4) + 1` to `const wIdx = (i % 8) + 1`. Also add `style={{ '--wi': i }}` and CSS `.idle-duration { z-index: calc(5 + var(--wi, 0)); }` so later workers' time badges stack above earlier badges on overlap, preventing the visual pile-up. Additionally, stagger y-offsets: wander paths for indices 5-8 should have slightly different y tracks (4-8% lower than their mirror counterparts) to prevent vertical overlap. WHY: Eight idle workers on a slow day should visually communicate 'the whole team is wandering.' Right now it communicates 'the floor has a rendering bug.' This is a pure visual polish fix with no data changes required.

ideas.title: Away Sign Long-Idle Escalation — The Note Ages With Time
ideas.description: The AwaySign component currently cycles through 6 messages (☕ Coffee run, 🧠 Deep think, etc.) rotating every 10 minutes based on `(seed + Math.floor(idleMinutes / 10)) % 6`. A worker idle for 3 hours shows the same register of note as one idle for 8 minutes. Add duration-based overrides with higher priority than the seed cycle: `if (idleMinutes >= 90) return ['🏠 WFH', 'maybe?']`, `else if (idleMinutes >= 45) return ['🍕 Still', 'at lunch']`, `else if (idleMinutes >= 25) return ['📞 Long', 'call...']`. These override the seed rotation entirely when thresholds are crossed. Additionally, apply a `.desk--long-idle` class (passed as a prop to the desk render) when `idleMinutes >= 45`. In CSS: `.desk--long-idle .desk-away-sign__note { filter: sepia(0.35) opacity(0.75); animation: note-curl 8s ease-in-out infinite; }` `@keyframes note-curl { 0%,100%{transform:rotate(-1deg)} 50%{transform:rotate(0.5deg)} }` — the note slowly curls as if it's been there all afternoon. Vibe override: when `vibe === 'on-fire'` and `idleMinutes >= 5`, ALL away signs show `['🚨 NEEDED', '!!']` in red text. WHY: The away sign is already rendered; it's already storytelling. Making it age with time turns an ambient decoration into a narrative clock. The curling note implies 'this person has been gone a while.' The on-fire crisis override is the best interaction point — when the building is on fire and half the team is away, every sticky note becomes a distress signal.

ideas.title: Deep Work Desk Aura — The Desk Knows Someone's in the Zone
ideas.description: The codebase already applies `char--deep-work` to a character avatar after 45 minutes of continuous work (Office.jsx line 400-402). But the DESK has no corresponding visual change — it looks identical at minute 5 and minute 180 of a session. Fix: in the desk render loop (JSX ~line 731), add `const isDeepWork = isWorking && occ.worker.updated_at && (Date.now() - new Date(occ.worker.updated_at).getTime()) > 45 * 60 * 1000` and include `isDeepWork ? ' desk--deep-work' : ''` in the className. CSS: `.desk--deep-work { border-color: #6d28d9 !important; transition: border-color 2s ease; }` `.desk--deep-work .desk__monitor { animation: deep-work-monitor 4s ease-in-out infinite !important; box-shadow: 0 0 16px rgba(109,40,217,0.7) !important; }` `@keyframes deep-work-monitor { 0%,100%{opacity:1; background:#0d0d1a;} 50%{opacity:0.65; background:#130d2a;} }` — a slow, breathing 4s pulse (versus the default 1.8s `monitor-blink`). The slower pulse reads as meditative focus rather than active typing. Add the deep-work session duration to `ghostDeskTitle`: instead of just 'Last seen: Xm ago', compute `const deepMins = Math.round((Date.now() - new Date(occ.worker.updated_at).getTime()) / 60000)` and set `title='⚡ Deep work: ${deepMins}m session'` when isDeepWork is true. WHY: Deep-work detection is already the codebase's most thoughtful observability feature — it identifies when someone has been heads-down for 45+ minutes. Currently it produces zero desk-level signal. A slowly breathing indigo desk across the office floor is the visual equivalent of someone putting headphones on: everyone knows not to interrupt. It creates a new visual vocabulary on the floor.

ideas.title: Cactus Vibe Barometer — The Hidden Easter Egg That Blooms
ideas.description: The cactus SVG at `left:92%, top:30%` already contains a yellow flower (`<circle cx=9 cy=6 r=2 fill=#fbbf24>`). This flower is completely invisible because it's tiny and static — it is an easter egg nobody knows exists. Give the cactus life. First, extract the cactus SVG into its own memo component `OfficeCactus({ vibeKey })`. Apply a `data-vibe={vibeKey}` attr to the root SVG element. Second, add a `className='cactus-flower'` to the flower circle so CSS can target it. CSS vibe states: `[data-vibe='crushing'] .cactus-flower { animation: cactus-bloom 1.4s ease-in-out infinite; filter: drop-shadow(0 0 4px rgba(251,191,36,0.9)); }` `@keyframes cactus-bloom { 0%,100%{r:2} 50%{r:3.5} }` (SVG animateTransform via CSS `transform-origin: center; animation: scale-bloom`). `[data-vibe='on-fire'] .cactus-flower { fill: #ef4444; filter: drop-shadow(0 0 7px rgba(239,68,68,0.85)); }` `[data-vibe='slow-day'] .cactus-flower { opacity: 0.2; }` `[data-vibe='after-hours'] .cactus-flower { fill: #818cf8; opacity: 0.5; transition: fill 2s ease; }` — a moonflower at night. Pass vibeKey from Office through to the cactus plant div. WHY: The floor already has one growing organism (Fern the ficus, which grows with vibeStreak). Adding a second living vibe-indicator creates the sense that the office environment is biologically responding to team energy. But the real value is the DISCOVERY — a user who notices the cactus blooming during a crushing day will feel like they found a secret. That moment of delight is worth more than any designed interaction.

ideas.title: The After-Hours Phantom — Someone's Always Working Late
ideas.description:
When `vibe === 'after-hours'`, the floor currently looks almost identical to slow-day with a blue tint and 'ZZZ' whiteboard. Nobody wanders. Monitors are dim. Bloberto is asleep. The floor feels abandoned. Add one haunting detail: a translucent silhouette of a roster member slowly crossing the dark floor. Implementation in Office.jsx: `const [phantom, setPhantom] = useState(null)`. A `useEffect` keyed on `vibe === 'after-hours'`: clears on vibe exit. When active, schedules the first phantom walk after `Math.random() * 60000 + 60000`ms (1-2min delay), then every `180000`ms (3min). Each fire picks `const ghost = DEFAULT_ROSTER[Math.floor(hourSeed % DEFAULT_ROSTER.length)]` (seeded by hour so it's the same person for the same hour). Sets `phantom = { worker: ghost, key: Date.now() }`. Renders: `{phantom && vibe === 'after-hours' &&
}`. CSS: `.char--phantom { position: absolute; pointer-events: none; opacity: 0; filter: blur(1.5px) grayscale(0.9) brightness(0.6); animation: phantom-walk 9s linear forwards; } @keyframes phantom-walk { 0%{left:-6%;opacity:0;} 10%{opacity:0.12;} 88%{opacity:0.12;} 100%{left:106%;opacity:0;} }`. After 9s, remove phantom from state via setTimeout in the same effect. WHY: After-hours is the state with the highest emotional potential and the lowest visual distinction. A ghostly silhouette of a colleague slowly crossing a dark office at midnight is a single moment that transforms the floor from 'screensaver' to 'short film.' The first time a user sees it, they will send a screenshot to someone. It is the kind of detail that makes a product feel loved.
